title: Fix PoisonError handling consistency in runtime
analysis: ConcurrentEvalAdapter and SharedPriorityPromoter have inconsistent PoisonError handling. The 'result extraction' paths (build_result_shared, into_result at lines 112-152) correctly use .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) to recover from poisoned locks, but accessor methods (values, take_results, skipped, is_dirty) and the evaluate() hot path use plain .unwrap(), which will cascade-panic if any concurrent task panics while holding a lock. SharedPriorityPromoter has the same issue across all 6 of its lock acquisitions. The fix is mechanical: replace all .unwrap() on Mutex::lock()/RwLock::read()/RwLock::write() with .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()). For testing, we need to deliberately poison locks and verify recovery. ConcurrentEvalAdapter's locks are behind Arc, so we can add #[cfg(test)] helpers that clone the relevant Arc and spawn a panicking thread to poison specific locks. SharedPriorityPromoter tests live in the same module so they can access the private `inner` field directly. Specifically: concurrent_eval.rs has 9 .unwrap() calls on lines 93, 98, 103, 176, 190, 220, 226, 242, 260 that need fixing; priority_promotion.rs has 6 .unwrap() calls on lines 121, 126, 131, 136, 147-148 that need fixing.

prerequisites.description: Add #[cfg(test)] poison helper methods to ConcurrentEvalAdapter (poison_skip_state, poison_results, poison_values, poison_snapshot_values) that clone the inner Arc, spawn a thread that acquires the lock and panics, thereby poisoning it. These are needed by integration tests in tests/concurrent_eval.rs to set up poisoned-lock scenarios.

steps.description: Write failing tests for adapter accessor methods recovering after lock poisoning. In tests/concurrent_eval.rs add: (1) `is_dirty_recovers_after_skip_state_poisoning` — poison skip_state, assert is_dirty() returns a bool without panicking; (2) `values_recovers_after_values_lock_poisoning` — poison values lock, assert values() returns a ValueMap; (3) `take_results_recovers_after_results_lock_poisoning` — poison results lock, assert take_results() returns a Vec; (4) `skipped_recovers_after_skip_state_poisoning` — poison skip_state, assert skipped() returns a HashSet. Each test creates a simple_setup adapter, poisons the relevant lock, then calls the accessor. These should FAIL because lines 93, 98, 103, 176 use .unwrap().

Fix .unwrap() on accessor methods in concurrent_eval.rs: line 93 values.read().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 98 results.lock().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 103 skip_state.lock().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 176 skip_state.lock().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()). All 4 step-1 tests should now pass.

Write failing test for evaluate() recovering with poisoned locks. In tests/concurrent_eval.rs add `evaluate_continues_after_lock_poisoning`: create adapter from simple_setup, poison the values RwLock, then call adapter.evaluate(b_node).await. Currently panics at line 190 (values.read().unwrap()). After fix, evaluate should complete (returning an EvalOutcome) without cascading panic. Also verify take_results() and build_result_shared() still work afterward (regression guard for already-fixed paths).

Fix .unwrap() in evaluate() method in concurrent_eval.rs: line 190 values.read().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 220 values.write().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 226 snapshot_values.write().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 242 skip_state.lock().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 260 results.lock().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()). Step-3 test should now pass.

Write failing test for SharedPriorityPromoter recovering after lock poisoning. In priority_promotion.rs mod tests add `shared_promoter_recovers_after_lock_poisoning`: create Arc<SharedPriorityPromoter>, register node_a at P3Speculative, spawn thread that acquires shared.inner.lock() and panics (private field accessible from unit tests in same module), then assert effective_priority() returns Some(P3Speculative), register() succeeds for new node, promote() succeeds, remove() succeeds, promote_for_demand() succeeds. Currently fails because all lock() calls use .unwrap().

Fix .unwrap() in SharedPriorityPromoter in priority_promotion.rs: line 121 inner.lock().unwrap() → .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()), line 126 same, line 131 same, line 136 same, lines 146-148 same. All 6 Mutex::lock() calls get the recovery pattern. Step-5 test should now pass. All existing tests continue to pass since unwrap_or_else is a superset of unwrap behavior for non-poisoned locks.

design_decisions.decision: Use .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) consistently for all lock acquisitions
design_decisions.rationale: This is the existing recovery pattern already used in build_result_shared() and into_result() (lines 112-152). It extracts the last valid data from a poisoned lock, allowing the system to continue with potentially stale but structurally valid state rather than cascading panics. This is the right choice for a CAD engine where a transient evaluation failure should not crash the entire system.

design_decisions.decision: Add #[cfg(test)] poison helper methods to ConcurrentEvalAdapter rather than exposing internal locks
design_decisions.rationale: Integration tests (tests/concurrent_eval.rs) cannot access private fields. Rather than making locks pub(crate) or restructuring, cfg(test) helpers provide a clean testing interface. Each helper clones the relevant Arc<Mutex/RwLock>, spawns a thread that acquires the lock and panics, poisoning it. This pattern is zero-cost in release builds.

design_decisions.decision: Test SharedPriorityPromoter poisoning via unit tests inside the module
design_decisions.rationale: Unit tests in priority_promotion.rs mod tests can access the private `inner` field directly, avoiding the need for test helper methods. This is simpler and matches the existing test pattern in that module which already uses std::sync::Arc and std::thread for concurrency tests.

design_decisions.decision: Do not convert to Result-returning APIs
design_decisions.rationale: The task scope is to make PoisonError handling consistent, not to change the API surface. Converting to Result<T, PoisonError> would require changing every call site and is a larger refactor. The unwrap_or_else pattern preserves the existing API while adding resilience.

reuse.how: Apply the same pattern to all remaining .unwrap() calls on lock acquisitions. This is the established convention in the codebase for PoisonError recovery.
Use the existing simple_setup() to create ConcurrentEvalAdapter instances for poison recovery tests, same as all other tests in the file.

reuse.what: make_node() test helper and existing SharedPriorityPromoter test patterns
reuse.how: Follow the existing Arc<SharedPriorityPromoter> + thread::spawn pattern (used in shared_promoter_promote_from_another_thread and shared_promoter_concurrent_promotes) for the poison recovery test.
Reference the existing pattern of creating evaluators that intentionally panic, used in rollback_on_task_panicked and repeated_error_then_success_cycle tests.
